content.full_text:
CALIFORNIA

==PERS

Memorandum
California Public Employees' Retirement System

To:     Kathryn Donovan
        General Counsel
        Legal Department
        Fair Political Practices Commission
        P.O. Box 807
        Sacramento, CA 95804

From: Board of Administration
       Lincoln Plaza, 400 P Street
       Sacramento, CA

Subject:

        Request for Written Advice (Gov. Code sec. 83114 (b) and Cal.
        Code Regs., tit. 2, sec. 18329 (b).)

        This is a request by the Public Employees' Retirement System
        (PERS) for written advice pursuant to Government Code section
        83114(b) and title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
        18329(b). A statement in compliance with Commission Regulation
        19329(2)(A) is attached as Appendix A.

Background of Issue Presented:

The issue of this opinion request was raised by a member of the
Health Benefits Advisory Council (HBAC), Ms. Joan Trauner. The
HBAC is a purely advisory body, created by resolution of the
PERS Board of Administration. The role of the HBAC is to
review matters pertinent to health benefits programs, and to
advise the PERS Board of its findings and recommendations. It
has no policy-making authority.

Ms. Trauner was appointed to the HBAC by the PERS Board of
Administration. Her occupation is as a partner with Coopers
and Lybrand, which company does business with PERS on a variety
of its business interests. She was appointed to the HBAC
because of her nationally recognized expertise in the health
care delivery industry. Her service on the HBAC is voluntary;
she receives no salary or benefits for her services, and is
only reimbursed for her expenses.

Issue Presented:

What activities should Ms. Trauner restrict to avoid
jeopardizing the ability of her employer, Coopers and Lybrand,
to bid on a PERS' contract?


PERS—ADM—76 (Rev. 8/86)

Kathryn Donovan                    2                    March 1, 1991


We were informed by Ms. Trauner that she is not in a position
which directly influences the outcome of our competitive
bidding process, nor is she in a position to influence the
contracting process in any other way. Yet, there is a concern
over any possible compromise with conflict of interest
principles. Therefore, we ask, in your view, what guidelines
should be applied, if any, to Ms. Trauner's activities for the
HBAC vis-à-vis her employer's relationship to PERS, which is
that of an independent contractor.

Our staff has proposed a draft response, which is included as
Attachment B, for your review and comment.

If you need further information, please contact Paul M. Ryan at
(916) 326-3666.

                         [signature]

RICHARD H. KOPPES
General Counsel

RHK:jks

Attachments

cc:  Craig Hartung
     Tom Elkin
     Margaret J. Hoehn

APPENDIX A

APPENDIX A

I, Richard H. Koppes, am the General Counsel for the Public
Employees' Retirement System (PERS). As such, I am responsible
for providing legal advice to the PERS Board, and its Committees,
Executive Staff, and the System Division Chiefs and Managers. I
am authorized to seek legal advice from the FPPC on behalf of the
Board and PERS officers and employees. This request for advice
will potentially affect the rights and obligations of the
following PERS Board Members and System staff:

PERS Board Members

Bill Ellis                          Thomas Clark
Jake Petrosino                    Madale Watson
William Crist                      Lori Ward
Robert Carlsen                    Gray Davis
Kurato Shimada                    David Tirapelle
Charles Valdes                    Kathleen Brown
Jack Wickware

Executive Staff

Dale Hanson, Chief Executive Officer
Basil Schwan, Assistant Executive Officer
Robert Walton, Assistant Executive Officer
Sandra Lund, Assistant Executive Officer
Robert Aguallo, Assistant Executive Officer
Tom Elkin, Assistant Executive Officer

Legal Staff

Richard H. Koppes, General Counsel
Kayla Gillan, Assistant General Counsel

Health Benefits Advisory Council:

Ms. Joan Trauner

The mailing address for these individuals for purposes of this
opinion request is:

PERS Legal Office
P.O. Box 942707
Sacramento, CA  94229-2707

_____________________               _____________________
DATE  3/1/91                        RICHARD H. KOPPES
                                    General Counsel

ATTACHMENT B

DRAFT

Avoidance of Conflict of Interest for a Member of the Health
Benefits Advisory Council: Joan Trauner Ph.D.

ISSUE

What activities should Ms. Trauner restrict to avoid jeopardizing
the ability of her employer, Coopers and Lybrand, to bid on a
PERS' contract?

CONCLUSION

Under the Public Employees' Medical and Hospital Care Act, Ms.
Trauner need not restrict her activities in her capacity as a
member of the Health Benefits Advisory Council.

Under the Fair Political Practices Act, however, Ms. Trauner does
need to restrict her activities in her capacity as a member of
the Health Benefits Advisory Council if she finds herself in a
position to influence official action with respect to her private
employment.

In general, Ms. Trauner, as a member of the Health Benefits
Advisory Council, functions only as one member of a body whose
function is purely advisory. That body has no decision-making
ability. It does not vote to award contracts, nor does it enter
into contracts because only the PERS Board has such authority.
The Council and its members are, however, in a position to
influence the Board's action in many ways. The Council's
specific function is to influence the Board of Administration.
It can and has made recommendations on specific firms and
organizations which would serve the Board's objectives, and it is
possible that recommendations may be made on firms and
organizations in which individual Council members have monetary
or employment interests or connections.

General guidelines for avoiding a conflict of interest are:

1)      Ms. Trauner should avoid participating in discussions
        in Council meetings which relate to contracting with
        consulting firms.

2)      She should avoid formal or informal attempts to
        persuade either the Council or any of its members, the
        Board or any of its members, and PERS staff to award or
        not award contracts in areas in which Coopers and
        Lybrand might compete.

3)    She should also decline, on any level of involvement,
to participate in discussions or to influence decisions
regarding contract terms or performance of Coopers and
Lybrand in its services to PERS.

4)    She should file with the Council a written notice of
her affiliation with Coopers and Lybrand, and indicate
that she will not participate in any discussions or
actions involving contract matters in which she has or
may have a financial interest.

DISCUSSION

No facts are provided in the question posed, except that it may
be inferred from the context of the question that it is limited
to activities of Ms. Joan Trauner relevant to PERS' contracting
with Coopers and Lybrand. Ms. Joan Trauner is both a partner in
the consulting firm of Coopers and Lybrand and appointed,
though voluntary, member of the Health Benefits Advisory Council
(Council). This analysis is therefore limited to the specific
conflicts of interest that may arise because of Ms. Trauner's
dual role as a Coopers and Lybrand partner and as a member of the
Council.

Potential conflicts revolve around an ability to influence the
making or keeping of contracts between the Board and Coopers and
Lybrand. Broadly speaking, a conflict of interest is a set of
circumstances arising apart from one's official duties which do,
or foreseeably could, affect a person's official duties. There
are a number of statutes relevant to conflicts of interest.
Various of these statutes will be discussed in turn.

Government Code section 22774¹

The Council is formed by Board action, pursuant to authority of
the Public Employees' Medical and Hospital Care Act (PEMHCA)
[sec. 22778] to assist the Board in its administration of PEMHCA.
Therefore, the first area of inquiry is under that Act, and there
is only one statute relevant to conflicts of interest.

Section 22774 provides:

     and "The board shall . . . approve health benefits plans
and may contract with carriers offering health benefits
plans.

     "Irrespective of the provisions of Sections 1090 and
1091 of this Code, the board member who is an officer of a
life insurer may participate in all board activities in
administering the provisions of this part, except that he

_________________
¹ All references in this opinion are to the California
Government Code.

shall not vote on the question of whether a contract should
be entered into or approval should be given concerning any
plan." (Emphasis added.)

The Council is not a statutorily created body. Rather, it is a
creation of the PERS Board of Administration. Its charter and
its operating guidelines are established solely by the Board.
Issues that it may consider in its official capacity are either
directed or approved by the Board. The Council has no authority
independent of that given to it by the Board.

The purpose of section 22774, cited and set forth above, is to
prevent an employee of an insurer who is also a member of the
PERS Board from voting on a contract between PERS and his
employer, or on some other contract that may have an effect on
the ability of his employer to contract, or continue to contract,
with PERS. As a Board Member, such a person has the authority,
absent some other limiting statute, to participate in the making
of decisions affecting contracting with his or her private
employer, but no authority to vote on such contract.

By its own terms, this statute does not extend to members of the
Health Benefits Advisory Council. A council member has no
authority to participate in deliberations about, or voting for,
contracts with his or her employer. Therefore, under this
statute, there is no prohibition against Ms. Trauner
participating in all deliberations of the Council.

Section 1090

Section 1090 provides:

"state . . . officers or employees shall not be
financially interested in any contract made by them in
their official capacity . . ." (Emphasis added.)

As discussed above, Council members have no authority to
negotiate or to enter into contracts. Neither the Council nor
its members can act with respect to contracts in an official
capacity. Therefore, it is irrelevant under this section if they
happen to have a financial interest in a contract, or potential
contract, between the PERS Board and his or her private employer.
Specific prohibitions and guidelines flow from sections 87100 and
87103, as discussed below:

Section 87100

Government Code section 87100 provides:

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way
attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest."

Unlike the two statutes cited above, this section has been
construed to apply to members of advisory groups to state
agencies in much the same way that the Health Benefits Advisory
Council is established to advise the Board of Administration.
The case of Commission on California State Government
Organization and Economy v. Fair Political Practices Commission
(1977) 74 Cal.App.3d 716 [142 Cal.Rptr. 468] held that
uncompensated, part-time appointed members of the commission were
not exempt from having to file financial disclosure statements
despite the fact that it was an advisory body. This was because
its activities were capable of influencing the actions of state
agencies, and members could influence such agencies with respect
to decisions regulating their private businesses. Like a
commissioner in this case, Ms. Trauner is an appointed member of
an advisory body.

Section 87103

Section 87103 specifies:

"An official has a financial interest in a
decision . . . if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or . . . on:

(a) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

(b) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

(c) Any source of income . . . aggregating
($250.00) . . .

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management . . ." (Emphasis added.)

This statute suggests that members of the Council must refrain
from those actions that could foreseeably affect their private
employer or their income from that employer. Under the Fair
Political Practices Act, this means that Council members must
refrain from influencing decisions that may foreseeably relate to
or affect their private employer. In Ms. Trauner's case, she
would be prohibited from influencing, or attempting to influence,
the Board with respect to contracts for which Coopers and Lybrand
compete. She would also be prohibited from influencing, or
attempting to influence other Council members or PERS staff in
this regard.

If the Board were to refer discussion of contracting with private
companies for any purpose to the Health Benefits Advisory

Council, Ms. Trauner could not participate in those
discussions.² In such a situation, it would be wise for Ms.
Trauner to refrain from participating in or influencing action on
such an issue since she might have a financial interest in the
outcome of such deliberations. Restraint in such situations
complies with Government Code sections 87100, 87103; with Board
Regulation 560.7; and also helps to avoid the appearance of any
impropriety. These provisions require disqualification from
participating in or influencing any action on a matter known to,
or suspected to have, a financial effect. More specifically,
these provisions require self disqualification on contract
matters.

In conclusion, general guidelines that may be used to avoid a
conflict of interest for Ms. Trauner include, but are not
necessarily limited to:

1)  Avoidance of discussions in Council meetings which
    relate to contracts with consulting firms.

2)  Avoidance of formal or informal attempts to persuade
    either the Council or any of its members, the Board or
    any of its members, or PERS staff to award or not award
    contracts in areas in which Coopers and Lybrand might
    compete.

3)  Declining to participate in discussions or to influence
    decisions regarding contract terms or performance of
    Coopers and Lybrand in its services to PERS.

In addition, Ms. Trauner should file with the Council a written
notice of her affiliation with Coopers and Lybrand, and indicate
that she will not participate in any discussions or actions
involving contract matters in which she has or may have a
financial interest.

If situations arise that are not addressed in this opinion, this
office should be consulted for clarification and guidance.

PMR:nt

cc: Executive Staff


_______________________

²  There is an exception pursuant to section 87101. If
a quorum could not be established without the participation
of a member who has a conflict of interest, such member may
participate in such discussion.

ATTACHMENT C

PERC

ATTACHMENT D

CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE FOR THE
PUBLIC EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM

APPENDIX


DESIGNATED POSITIONS                          ASSIGNED
                                             DISCLOSURE
                                             CATEGORY

BOARD MEMBERS & EXECUTIVE OFFICERS
Board Members                                      1

Executive Officer                                  1

Assistant Executive Officers                       1

INVESTMENT OFFICE
Investment Manager                                 1

Principal Investment Officer (all classes)        1

LEGAL OFFICE
General Counsel                                   1

Staff Counsel (all classes)                       1

Services Division                                              1

Chief, Information and Program Development Division            1

Program & Development Manager                                 2

Assistant Chief, Operations Support
Services Division                                                 1

INTERNAL AUDITOR
Chief Internal Auditor                                            1

CONTRACTS FOR SERVICES
Consultants*                                                      1




*Consultants shall be included in the list of designated
employees and shall disclose pursuant to the broadest
disclosure category in the code subject to the following
limitation:

The Executive Officer may determine in writing that a particular
consultant, although a "designated position," is hired to
perform a range of duties that are limited in scope and thus is
not required to fully comply with the disclosure requirements
described in this section. Such written determination shall
include a description of the consultant's duties and, based upon
that description, a statement of the extent of disclosure
requirements. The Executive Officer's determination is a public
record and shall be retained for public inspection in the same
manner and location as this Conflict of Interest Code.

DISCLOSURE CATEGORIES
Category 1
Designated individuals in Category 1 must report:

     All investments and business positions in business entities, and income from sources which are of the type (1) to contract with PERS, or (2) in which funds administered by the Board may be invested (including securities, real estate and business entities).

     All interests in real estate co-owned with, or purchased from, the above sources.

Category 2
Designated individuals in Category 2 must report:

     All investments and business positions in business entities, and income from sources which are of the type to contract with PERS to provide health care services.

Category 3
Designated individuals in Category 3 must report:

     All investments and business positions in business entities, and income from sources which are of the type to sell, rent or lease data processing equipment, hardware, software, facilities, supplies or services to PERS.

ATTACHMENT E

PUBLIC EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM
CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE

560. General Provisions

    The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000, et
seq., requires state and local government agencies to adopt and
promulgate Conflict of Interest Codes.  The Fair Political
Practices Commission has adopted a regulation, 2 Cal. Code of
Regs.  Section 18730, which contains the terms of a standard
Conflict of Interest Code, which can be incorporated by
reference, and which may be amended by the Fair Political
Practices Commission to conform to amendments in the Political
Reform Act after public notice and hearings.  Therefore, the
terms of 2, Cal. Code of Regs.  Section 18730 and any
amendments to it duly adopted by the Fair Political Practices
Commission, along with the attached Appendix in which officials
and employees are designated and disclosure categories are set
forth, are hereby incorporated by reference and constitute the
Conflict of Interest Code of the Public Employees' Retirement
System, except as provided below.

    Pursuant to Section 4 of the standard Code, designated
employees shall file statements of economic interests with the
agency.  Upon receipt of the statements of Board Members and the
Executive Officer, the agency shall make and retain a copy and
forward the original of these statements to the Fair Political
Practices Commission.

Exceptions: As provided in Section 1 of the standard Code,
2, Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18730 (b)(1), the
definitions contained in the Political Reform Act of 1974 shall
apply to the terms used in this Code except that "jurisdiction"
shall not be limited to the State, for the purposes of disclosure
and disqualification, for designated employees assigned to
Category 1 of the Appendix.

sections.question:
What activities should Ms. Trauner restrict to avoid jeopardizing the ability of her employer, Coopers and Lybrand, to bid on a PERS' contract?

We were informed by Ms. Trauner that she is not in a position which directly influences the outcome of our competitive bidding process, nor is she in a position to influence the contracting process in any other way. Yet, there is a concern over any possible compromise with conflict of interest principles. Therefore, we ask, in your view, what guidelines should be applied, if any, to Ms. Trauner's activities for the HBAC vis-a-vis her employer's relationship to PERS, which is that of an independent contractor.

Our staff has proposed a draft response, which is included as Attachment B, for your review and comment.

If you need further information, please contact Paul M. Ryan at (916) 326-3666.

[signature]
RICHARD H. KOPPES
sections.facts:
of Issue Presented:

The issue of this opinion request was raised by a member of the Health Benefits Advisory Council (HBAC), Ms. Joan Trauner. The HBAC is a purely advisory body, created by resolution of the PERS Board of Administration. The role of the HBAC is to review matters pertinent to health benefits programs, and to advise the PERS Board of its findings and recommendations. It has no policy-making authority.

Ms. Trauner was appointed to the HBAC by the PERS Board of Administration. Her occupation is as a partner with Coopers and Lybrand, which company does business with PERS on a variety of its business interests. She was appointed to the HBAC because of her nationally recognized expertise in the health care delivery industry. Her service on the HBAC is voluntary; she receives no salary or benefits for her services, and is only reimbursed for her expenses.
sections.analysis:
No facts are provided in the question posed, except that it may be inferred from the context of the question that it is limited to activities of Ms. Joan Trauner relevant to PERS' contracting with Coopers and Lybrand. Ms. Joan Trauner is both a partner in the consulting firm of Coopers and Lybrand and an appointed, though voluntary, member of the Health Benefits Advisory Council (Council). This analysis is therefore limited to the specific conflicts of interest that may arise because of Ms. Trauner's dual role as a Coopers and Lybrand partner and as a member of the Council.

Potential conflicts revolve around an ability to influence the making or keeping of contracts between the Board and Coopers and Lybrand. Broadly speaking, a conflict of interest is a set of circumstances arising apart from one's official duties which do, or foreseeably could, affect a person's official duties. There are a number of statutes relevant to conflicts of interest. Various of these statutes will be discussed in turn.

Government Code section 22774¹

The Council is formed by Board action, pursuant to authority of the Public Employees' Medical and Hospital Care Act (PEMHCA) [sec. 22778] to assist the Board in its administration of PEMHCA. Therefore, the first area of inquiry is under that Act, and there is only one statute relevant to conflicts of interest.

Section 22774 provides:

"The board shall . . . approve health benefits plans and may contract with carriers offering health benefits plans.

"Irrespective of the provisions of Sections 1090 and 1091 of this Code, the board member who is an officer of a life insurer may participate in all board activities in administering the provisions of this part, except that he

¹ All references in this opinion are to the California Government Code.

The Council is not a statutorily created body. Rather, it is a creation of the PERS Board of Administration. Its charter and its operating guidelines are established solely by the Board. Issues that it may consider in its official capacity are either directed or approved by the Board. The Council has no authority independent of that given to it by the Board.

The purpose of section 22774, cited and set forth above, is to prevent an employee of an insurer who is also a member of the PERS Board from voting on a contract between PERS and his employer, or on some other contract that may have an effect on the ability of his employer to contract, or continue to contract, with PERS. As a Board Member, such a person has the authority, absent some other limiting statute, to participate in the making of decisions affecting contracting with his or her private employer, but no authority to vote on such contract.

By its own terms, this statute does not extend to members of the Health Benefits Advisory Council. A council member has no authority to participate in deliberations about, or voting for, contracts with his or her employer. Therefore, under this statute, there is no prohibition against Ms. Trauner participating in all deliberations of the Council.

Section 1090

Section 1090 provides:

"state . . . officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity . . ." (Emphasis added.)

As discussed above, Council members have no authority to negotiate or to enter into contracts. Neither the Council nor its members can act with respect to contracts in an official capacity. Therefore, it is irrelevant under this section if they happen to have a financial interest in a contract, or potential contract, between the PERS Board and his or her private employer. Specific prohibitions and guidelines flow from sections 87100 and 87103, as discussed below:

Section 87100

Government Code section 87100 provides:

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

Unlike the two statutes cited above, this section has been construed to apply to members of advisory groups to state agencies in much the same way that the Health Benefits Advisory Council is established to advise the Board of Administration. The case of Commission on California State Government Organization and Economy v. Fair Political Practices Commission (1977) 74 Cal.App.3d 716 [142 Cal.Rptr. 468] held that uncompensated, part-time appointed members of the commission were not exempt from having to file financial disclosure statements despite the fact that it was an advisory body. This was because its activities were capable of influencing the actions of state agencies, and members could influence such agencies with respect to decisions regulating their private businesses. Like a commissioner in this case, Ms. Trauner is an appointed member of an advisory body.

Section 87103

Section 87103 specifies:

"An official has a financial interest in a decision . . . if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or . . . on:

(a) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

(b) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

(c) Any source of income . . . aggregating ($250.00) . . .

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. . ." (Emphasis added.)

This statute suggests that members of the Council must refrain from those actions that could foreseeably affect their private employer or their income from that employer. Under the Fair Political Practices Act, this means that Council members must refrain from influencing decisions that may foreseeably relate to or affect their private employer. In Ms. Trauner's case, she would be prohibited from influencing, or attempting to influence, the Board with respect to contracts for which Coopers and Lybrand compete. She would also be prohibited from influencing, or attempting to influence other Council members or PERS staff in this regard.

If the Board were to refer discussion of contracting with private companies for any purpose to the Health Benefits Advisory

Council, Ms. Trauner could not participate in those discussions.2 In such a situation, it would be wise for Ms. Trauner to refrain from participating in or influencing action on such an issue since she might have a financial interest in the outcome of such deliberations. Restraint in such situations complies with Government Code sections 87100, 87103; with Board Regulation 560.7; and also helps to avoid the appearance of any impropriety. These provisions require disqualification from participating in or influencing any action on a matter known to, or suspected to have, a financial effect. More specifically, these provisions require self disqualification on contract matters.

In conclusion, general guidelines that may be used to avoid a conflict of interest for Ms. Trauner include, but are not necessarily limited to:

1) Avoidance of discussions in Council meetings which relate to contracts with consulting firms.

2) Avoidance of formal or informal attempts to persuade either the Council or any of its members, the Board or any of its members, or PERS staff to award or not award contracts in areas in which Coopers and Lybrand might compete.

3) Declining to participate in discussions or to influence decisions regarding contract terms or performance of Coopers and Lybrand in its services to PERS.

In addition, Ms. Trauner should file with the Council a written notice of her affiliation with Coopers and Lybrand, and indicate that she will not participate in any discussions or actions involving contract matters in which she has or may have a financial interest.

If situations arise that are not addressed in this opinion, this office should be consulted for clarification and guidance.

PMR:nt

cc: Executive Staff

2 There is an exception pursuant to section 87101. If a quorum could not be established without the participation of a member who has a conflict of interest, such member may participate in such discussion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What activities should Ms. Trauner restrict to avoid jeopardizing the ability of her employer, Coopers and Lybrand, to bid on a PERS' contract?

We were informed by Ms. Trauner that she is not in a position which directly influences the outcome of our competitive bidding process, nor is she in a position to influence the contracting process in any other way. Yet, there is a concern over any possible compromise with conflict of interest principles. Therefore, we ask, in your view, what guidelines should be applied, if any, to Ms. Trauner's activities for the HBAC vis-a-vis her employer's relationship to PERS, which is that of an independent contractor.

Our staff has proposed a draft response, which is included as Attachment B, for your review and comment.

If you need further information, please contact Paul M. Ryan at (916) 326-3666.

[signature]
RICHARD H. KOPPES
embedding.first_500_words: **Memorandum** California Public Employees' Retirement System To: Kathryn Donovan General Counsel Legal Department Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 From: Board of Administration Lincoln Plaza, 400 P Street Sacramento, CA Subject: Request for Written Advice (Gov. Code sec. 83114 (b) and Cal. Code Regs., tit. 2, sec. 18329 (b).) This is a request by the Public Employees' Retirement System (PERS) for written advice pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b) and title 2, California Code of Regulations, section 18329(b). A statement in compliance with Commission Regulation 19329(2)(A) is attached as Appendix A. Background of Issue Presented: The issue of this opinion request was raised by a member of the Health Benefits Advisory Council (HBAC), Ms. Joan Trauner. The HBAC is a purely advisory body, created by resolution of the PERS Board of Administration. The role of the HBAC is to review matters pertinent to health benefits programs, and to advise the PERS Board of its findings and recommendations. It has no policy-making authority. Ms. Trauner was appointed to the HBAC by the PERS Board of Administration. Her occupation is as a partner with Coopers and Lybrand, which company does business with PERS on a variety of its business interests. She was appointed to the HBAC because of her nationally recognized expertise in the health care delivery industry. Her service on the HBAC is voluntary; she receives no salary or benefits for her services, and is only reimbursed for her expenses. Issue Presented: What activities should Ms. Trauner restrict to avoid jeopardizing the ability of her employer, Coopers and Lybrand, to bid on a PERS' contract? We were informed by Ms. Trauner that she is not in a position which directly influences the outcome of our competitive bidding process, nor is she in a position to influence the contracting process in any other way. Yet, there is a concern over any possible compromise with conflict of interest principles. Therefore, we ask, in your view, what guidelines should be applied, if any, to Ms. Trauner's activities for the HBAC vis-a-vis her employer's relationship to PERS, which is that of an independent contractor. Our staff has proposed a draft response, which is included as Attachment B, for your review and comment. If you need further information, please contact Paul M. Ryan at (916) 326-3666. [signature] RICHARD H. KOPPES General Counsel RHK:jks Attachments cc: Craig Hartung Tom Elkin Margaret J. Hoehn The image shows a page with the text "APPENDIX A" centered at the top in bold, black letters. The rest of the page is blank and colored in a light green or mint color. APPENDIX A I, Richard H. Koppes, am the General Counsel for the Public Employees' Retirement System (PERS). As such, I am responsible for providing legal advice to the PERS Board, and its Committees, Executive Staff, and the System Division Chiefs and Managers. I am authorized to seek legal advice from the FPPC on behalf of the Board and PERS officers and employees. This request for advice
